coredata.dc:title: Surface activity of N-graphene quantum dot in presence of CTAB surfactant
coredata.dc:creator: Koolivand, Hadis
Mazinani, Saeedeh
Sharif, Farhad
coredata.dc:description: An interface can be subtly engineered utilizing nanoparticle-surfactant interactions by controlling surface activity and surface charge balancing. In this research, the effect of N-doped Graphene Quantum Dot particles (N-GQDs) and Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide surfactant (CTAB) presence in the air/water interface is explored. We found on the study of interplay of N-GQD with CTAB that N-GQDs are surface active particles. Thereafter, the effect of concentration ratio of CTAB to N-GQD on surface tension and elasticity is investigated up to critical micelle concentration (CMC). The results demonstrate the surface activity of N-GQDs when there is no surfactant molecules (1 wt% N-GQD, surface tension (ST) ∼53). In addition, the results manifest that due to CTAB molecules' attachment to the N-GQD particles, more efficient surface-active mixtures are created. At higher surfactant loadings, the excess CTAB monomers compete with CTAB/ N-GQDs mixtures to reach the interfaces, causing mixture to come back into the bulk of liquid verified by elasticity evaluations. The preparation of N-GQDs and as a result its mixture includes economical and easy experimental protocols, and according to the synergistic effects defeat the individual components constraints i.e. –at 1% N-GQD+0.25 CMC CTAB the dynamic surface tension lies below pure CTAB CMC line.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB)
Interfacial rheology
Dynamic adsorption
Profile tensiometry analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dynamic surface tension of pure N-GQDs
Equilibrium surface tension & elasticity of pure N-GQDs
Dynamic interfacial tension of CTAB and N­GQDs mixture

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The pure N-GQD quantum dots dynamic surface tensions in the concentration span range of 0 to 1 wt% (A) Surface tension recordings in the course of sinusoidal drop surface area/ volume perturbation (8 percent of the initially created drop volume after reaching an equilibrium state).
(A)Equilibrium surface tension and Elasticity (at F = 0.02 Hz) of the pure N-GQD as a function of the nanoparticle concentration (The images of the drop during this experiment are shown).

Dynamic surface tensions of the (A) 0.092 mM, (B) 0.23 mM, and (C) 0.92 mM of CTAB with N-GQD concentration at the range of 0 to 1 wt%, oscillations during approaching to the equilibrium for tracing elasticity evolution.
The proposed mechanism of the N-GQD, CTAB/N-GQD adsorption at the interface of gas/liquid with various molecular ratio of surfactant-nanoparticles.

A) The protocol of droplet area compression and expansion and the recorded surface tension for CTAB solution at 3.6 × 10−1 mM- 0.4 wt.% N-GQDs, where the highlighted zone refers to the programmed drop regulation, B) The hysteresis graph obtained from plot a), in which γ0 was gained from the initial surface tension at the border of the shaded area.
Elasticity (at F = 0.02 Hz) for N-GQD and CTAB mixtures as a function of the number of CTAB molecules to N-GQDs nanoparticles number.
ζ-Potential of the CTAB/N-GQDs dispersions (Figure S3. in supplementary).
Surfactant to N-GQD nanoparticle molecular ratio at different nanoparticles concentrations, at each concentration overall N-GQD numbers is calculated.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aSurfactant numbers for all above solutions is 4.4 × 1014 (for CTAB concentration 9.2 × 10−2 mM).

head.ce:title: Surface activity of N-graphene quantum dot in presence of CTAB surfactant

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Engineering and Color Technology, Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Engineering and Color Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Polymer Engineering and Color Technology, Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: New Technologies Research Center (NTRC), Amirkabir University of Technology, 424 Hafez Ave., Tehran 15875-4413, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNew Technologies Research Center (NTRC), Amirkabir University of Technology, 424 Hafez Ave., Tehran 15875-4413, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An interface can be subtly engineered utilizing nanoparticle-surfactant interactions by controlling surface activity and surface charge balancing. In this research, the effect of N-doped Graphene Quantum Dot particles (N-GQDs) and Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide surfactant (CTAB) presence in the air/water interface is explored. We found on the study of interplay of N-GQD with CTAB that N-GQDs are surface active particles. Thereafter, the effect of concentration ratio of CTAB to N-GQD on surface tension and elasticity is investigated up to critical micelle concentration (CMC). The results demonstrate the surface activity of N-GQDs when there is no surfactant molecules (1 wt% N-GQD, surface tension (ST) ∼53). In addition, the results manifest that due to CTAB molecules' attachment to the N-GQD particles, more efficient surface-active mixtures are created. At higher surfactant loadings, the excess CTAB monomers compete with CTAB/ N-GQDs mixtures to reach the interfaces, causing mixture to come back into the bulk of liquid verified by elasticity evaluations. The preparation of N-GQDs and as a result its mixture includes economical and easy experimental protocols, and according to the synergistic effects defeat the individual components constraints i.e. –at 1% N-GQD+0.25 CMC CTAB the dynamic surface tension lies below pure CTAB CMC line.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Complex fluid mixtures, such as nanosheets-surfactant systems, have gained research interests due to their diverse industrial applications [1]. On the other hand, stable aqueous foam production has a growing and extensive industrial usage such as waste-water treatment, food, cosmetics, etc. [2–7]. Sheet-like nanoparticles and surfactant interaction are a decisive factor for adjusting emulsions, foams and suspensions properties, stabilities, and surface modifications. Therefore, we seek to comprehend surfactant-nanosheets interplays both in the liquid bulk and air/water interface. Generally, graphene nanoparticles are individually surface active at the air/water interface, and their existence in surfactant mixtures can significantly influence and intensify the interfacial rheological properties [1,8]. These surface modifiers can either dramatically augment surfactants' surface activity or easily become surface active due to the surfactant molecules' adsorption process. Various pieces of literature demonstrate this behavior for nanoparticle and surfactant mixed solutions [9–11]. It has also been pointed out that multiple mixtures of nanoparticles and surfactants lead to the notable development in adsorption dynamics and rheology of the interface [9].

Previous studies have shown some organized research on the nanoparticle and surfactant combined systems at the liquid/gas interface. i.e. Arriaga et al.[12]. explored the surface characteristics of silica and short-chain amphiphile mixed dispersions. They signified that at the 0.5 wt% of silica and higher, there has been a critical value for the cooperative interaction among particles and amine molecules [12,13]. Most recent studies mention that collective behaviors and electrostatic interplays exist between surfactant and nanoparticle result in improved properties of the hybrid solutions such as high foam/emulsions stability. These interactions heighten the surfactant adsorption onto the nanoparticle and nanosheet surface and enhance its hydrophobicity [1,8,14,15].

In our recent research [1], a mixed dispersion of SDS (anionic surfactant) and GO (Graphene oxide - amphiphilic nanoparticle) is investigated at various molecular ratios. It was shown that there has been a critical value for the multitudes of surfactant monomers per GO nanosheet (higher than 160) to convert the amphiphilic particles to more hydrophilic ones.

In the examined GO-SDS system, a similar charged molecule system, the SDS adsorption was mainly on the particle surface. The complex adsorption occurs on the interface, and the complex exhibited more surface activity than the pure particles and surfactants. The stabilization of the GOCTAB system was not possible and the GO became rapidly unstable, and it was not easy to test it. There has been a suggestion to select an equivalent structure like graphene quantum dot and to employ the n-doped protocols to resolve the system stability problem. Despite being n-doped, the nanoparticle size effect could be analyzed, which is the primary purpose of this study.

This study aims to explore the N-GQDs, and CTAB interplays at the air/liquid interface at various ratios of surfactant to nanosheets. Mixtures of CTAB with the small size nanoparticles of GQDs (about 5 nm size) are studied. The dynamic surface tension of the hybrid nanoparticle/ surfactant systems was measured at different molecular ratios. Apart from the dynamic surface behaviors, the interfacial layer response to low and high amplitude oscillations provides information about the mechanism and the complex layer formation at the interface. The dilational viscoelasticity results are primarily evaluated when surface area perturbation is small. The dilational viscoelasticity results exhibit the restructuring of the mixed film of surfactant/ nanoparticle complexes [16]. Higher frequency analysis may provide more insight into changes in the particles and complexes and their interplays at the interface. It will deepen our understanding of the interfacial structural behaviors and comprehension of the nanoparticle/ surfactant macroscopic structures formation [12,17-23].

Although graphene-based materials have gained tremendous attention during the last decade, [15] understanding the behavior of GQDs containing systems is at this preliminary stage of study and requires more research. In this study, the behavior GQDs/CTAB mixture as surfactant are deeply studied by careful measurements.

In this work, various solutions with different weight percent of N-GQDs nanosheets, mixed with CTAB surfactant were prepared. These solutions devised to be in a wide range of CTAB concentrations below the CMC. The chemical synthesizing procedure, preparation protocols of samples, and experimental methods are discussed below.

N-GQDs nanosheet particles are synthesized according to the following procedure demonstrated in the next section, with about 5 nm diameter size, which is verified by the characterizations test presented in the following sections. The cationic surfactant, CTAB (MW = 336.40 g/mol, purity of > 99%) was purchased from Sigma- Aldrich. The respective surfactants' stock solutions were prepared in pH=5.6 using ultra-pure MilliQ water (resistivity = 18.2 M_ cm).

In this study, all the materials were obtained from Merck and Sigma Aldrich, and they have been utilized without further purification. The N-GQDs nanoparticles were fabricated via Qu et al. [24] derived procedure. Briefly, at first citric acid (4 gr) and urea (3.5 gr) were mixed with distilled water (100 ml). Then the prepared sample solution was relocated to an autoclave and for 2 h warmed at 160 °C. Subsequently, the stock material was maintained in an oven to dehydrate the mixture at 80 °C, and afterward, N-GQDs nanoparticle powder was synthesized.

After N-GQDs preparation, the mixture of N-GQDs\CTAB at various nanoparticle and surfactant concentrations was dissolved in 50 ml of distilled water. And lastly, the solution was sonicated for 10 min. The dispersions of CTAB\N-GQDs were processed in two stages by N-GQDs nanoparticle source material dilution. In the first lap, the primary N-GQDs nanodispersion was diluted to the double aimed concentration by adding pure water. In the second lap, the surfactant solution with the same volume with two times the aimed concentration was added to the diluted solution in a dropwise manner. All along with the solution preparation, the mixture was gently agitated to omit the aggregation of particles. Ultimately, the eventual dispersion of the CTAB- N-GQDs was sonicated in an ultrasonic bath.

Ziess E900 instrument was utilized for gaining the TEM images of N-GQDs for Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) tests. For this reason, a specified amount of sample solution was placed in an ultrasonic bath. After that, a tiny drop of solution was deposited on a TEM grid; finally, the solution drop on TEM grid was dried by air. Tecnai G2 F20 instrument was employed for the High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) images of N-GQDs, which were taken place at 200 kV. Bruker multi-8mode device was used for the recording of the Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) images. Holland Philips tool was employed for XRD pattern determination of specimens, which were processed using x-ray diffraction (Cu Ka, K = 1.5406 ˚A). Tensor 27, Bruker Inc. device was applied for Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) evaluation in the range of 500 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The CTAB/ N-GQD solutions Zeta potential was measured by using the WALLIS Zeta Potential electrophoresis instrument of CORDOUAN, which was equipped with a 20 mW diode at 635 nm connected to an automatic optical attenuation device. The Zeta potential measured values were verified using the equation of Helmholtz-Smoluchowski via the measurement of particle mobility.

The dynamic surface tension and dilational viscoelasticity at the air/water interface were measured by a dynamic pendant drop tensiometry method. The drop profile tensiometry experimental setup (PAT1, Sinterface Technology, Germany) and theoretical calculation background were thoroughly defined somewhere else [25]. Concisely, the measurement protocol include drop profile shape acquisition, computer digitization, and Gauss-Laplace equation fitting. The aqueous solution drop was established at the stainless steel needle tip by a computer-driven plunger pump in an empty glassy cuvette. The PAT1 controlled injection setup facilitates dynamically programmed evaluations by retaining the volume/surface area of drop at a constant value. Afterward, the interface layer elasticity is measured by applying appropriate perturbations of the drop surface and simultaneously interpreting the surface tension feedback using a Fourier transform procedure. In favor of linear oscillation feedback, the frequency and the amplitude of the perturbations span were separately adjusted to be between 0.01 and 0.1 Hz and drop surface area of 8% [26,27]. Surface tension variations and the Laplacian drop shape dissimilarities were probed by implementing different compression and expansion amplitudes in distinct protocols to the droplet surface volume.

The N-GQDs structure and morphology are fully outlined in supplementary. TEM images of the N-GQD shown in Figure S1A prove that synthesized N-GQDs have a uniform size span of 2.5 to 8.2 nm diameters. The N-GQDs average diameter sizes are approximately 4.5 nm (Figure S1B). The corresponding HR-TEM images verified that N-GQDs possess a remarkably crystalline structure. The lattice fringe distance is measured to be 0.24 nm (Figure S1C), which attributes to the 1120 graphite crystalline phase. The images from scanning probe microscopy (SPM) (Figure S1D) also confirm the N-GQDs uniformity. The profile of SPM shows that the mean topographic N-GQDs height is in the range of 0.5 to 2.0 nm, indicating 1–5 graphene layers [28–31]. The N-GQDS XRD pattern is given in Figure S2A. N-GQDs declare a broad peak at 2θ = 29°, with approximately 0.31 nm d-spacing. The 002 of bulk graphite shows a diffraction peak of 2θ = 26.3 and 0.33 nm of d-spacing. Furthermore, the width of this peak is attributed to the small N-GQDs particle size [24]. The FTIR spectra of N-GQDs are depicted in Figure S2B. Two broad bands of spectrum are detected in FTIR graph of N-GQDS, which are at 3411 and 3199 cm −1, signifying the OH and NH groups existence in N-GQDS structure separately. Furthermore, peaks of 1574, 1340, and 1328 cm−1 correspond to the C = C, C = O, and CN bands, representing the attendance of the carbon element in the N-GQDS structure [6 – 8]

Generally, the nanoparticles lean to agglomerate at the higher attractive forces; thereafter, the dispersion moves to unstable state, and flocculation happens due to the particles attachment. So, as long as the surfactant/nanoparticles electrostatic repulsive force, hydrophobic interplays, and van der Waals attractive force balance the aggregation extent is easily controlled. It is obvious in our case the surface charge density of N-GQD regulates the amount of CTAB adsorption [28]. So, one of the major factors of the kinetic stability of nanofluids is surface electric charges. Zeta potential is a prominent method for examining the adsorption occurrence on the N-GQDs nanosheet surface [32]. According to Table 1 , N-GQD Zeta potential is measured to be −34.01 mV (negative value), mainly rooted in phenol, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functional groups' negative charge.

The dispersion Zeta potential changes by the addition of CTAB and N-GQDs. Zeta potential increases by the addition of CTAB, due to the positive charge of CTAB. At low concentration of CTAB, the adsorption of surfactant on N-GQDs sheets happens. Effective charge variation on the N-GQD surface proves that as the CTAB concentration increases, the higher adsorption on the N-GQDs surface occurs, which subsequently declines the N-GQDs effective charge. The CTAB positive charge nature and ligand-wrapped reaction are the dominant factors that lead to this behavior, so hydrophilic N-GQDs turn to hydrophobic particles [33].

This research evaluated a wide range of dynamic surface properties at the air/water interface for various weight percent of N-GQDs and CTAB CMC (Critical Micelle Concentration) fractions. The first section summarizes the surface property evaluations, including dilational elasticity and dynamic surface tension. The second section briefly describes the mechanism of N-GQDs particle adsorption at the air/water interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic surface tension of pure N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the first series of experiments, dynamic surface tension variations at the air/water interface in the span of 0.01 to 1 wt% of N-GQDs were measured (surfactant-free samples). It must be noted that at the t = t0 = 0 the measurement is initiated immediately after the droplet is created. Section 2.4.1 summarizes the measurement procedure, which takes about 1800 to 2000 s. Fig. 1 depicts the dynamic surface tension of pure N-GQDs at the air/water interface with various weight percentages. Throughout the experiment it is confirmed that significant quantities of N-GQDs particles are adsorbed at the interface based on their weight percent due to the slow dynamic of adsorption (that is inferred from the decreasing surface tension and the trend followed at t = 0). As in Fig. 1A, the pure N-GQDs samples' surface tension depends on the droplet surface freshness at all concentrations. The surface tension reduction is notable, decreasing about 20 mN/m unit at the 1 wt% of n-GQDs. These observations are in agreement with the similar studies of the oil/water/carbon quantum dots systems [15]. All the experimental evidence certifies that the N-GQDs particles are surface-active and readily adsorbed at the interface. The N-GQD amphiphilic nature and the increased mobility of small particles relative to surfactant monomers and Brownian motion are the main reasons for the N-GQDs adsorption. In addition, the presence of N-GQD particles at the interface of gas/ liquid confirms the N-GQDs surface activity. The surface behavior of hydrophilic nanoparticles are mainly independents of their applied weight percentages and as a result no nanoparticle adsorption occurred [4]. When the interactions of particles have steric force nature, no surface tension variations take place with particle adsorption [34]. This behavior is different from the ones observed in partial hydrophobic particles, which are also presented in this research. Their molecular interplays are determined chiefly by repulsive electrostatic and attractive capillary forces. Particle interactions lead to fluid surface curvature around the particle, which are caused by chemical inhomogeneity of particle surface or irregular particle aggregate shape which results in local wettability on particle surfaces.

Consequently, it leads to the molecule's interplays. In all nanoparticle concentrations, a detectable shift of surface tension was recognized while the data tracking process performed up to 2000 s of measurement. In the span of 0.01 to 1 wt.% of N-GQDs, the dynamic surface tension was evaluated, and it experienced 5–18 mN/m of decrease in this span. At the 1 wt.% of N-GQDs, the surface tension decreases down to around 55 mN/m.

Hence, particle adsorption can be recognized by the surface tension variation with time, where kinetics is ordinarily slowed down. The diffusion-controlled mechanism of nanoparticle adsorption is also activated as Kutuzov et al. [35] for particles less than 10 nm size have demonstrated. Adsorption kinetics involves steric/electrostatic interactions and express related energy barriers.

These systems showed a moderate response to the surface area/ volume variations (Fig. 1B). Surface pressure variations amid short sinusoidal altitude of perturbation were 8 percent of the initially created drop volume after reaching an equilibrium state in all samples. The change in the surface tension for the systems of 1 wt.% of N-GQDs is almost 4.0 mN/m.

The dynamic surface tension measurement proceeds until the system reaches to an equilibrium state. The sinusoidal changes in surface tension at this state may be attributed to the adsorption and desorption of the N-GQDs from the droplet's bulk to the surface of the droplet.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Equilibrium surface tension & elasticity of pure N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2 reveals how N-GQDs addition affects the surface properties of nanoparticle samples. Fig. 2 and Figure S4 illustrate the equilibrium surface tension, Laplacian fitting deviation error STDE, and elasticity values of pure N-GQDs using drop perturbations protocols at the frequency of F = 0.02 Hz and weight percent of 0–1 wt%. As Fig. 2 depicted, the elasticity data shows increasing trend for N-GQDs specimens of 0 to 0.01 wt% and touch 50 mN/m. In more concentrated samples from 0.01 to 1 wt% of N-GQDs, a plateau in the elasticity changes is seen, and there is no substantial variation in elasticity. The equilibrium surface tensions of 0 to 0.01 wt% pure N-GQDs samples have identical trends, i.e., by adding N-GQDs to 0.01 wt%, the surface tension decreased to 60 mN/m, and finally, a plateau is observed. Three different zones in this graph are distinguishable:
I)
In the first zone, very little N-GQD is added to the solution, which is readily adsorbed at the gas/ liquid interface. Due to the particle's surface activity, the surface tension decreases by adding N-GQDs, and a significant change in surface tension is observed till 0.01 wt% of particles.
II)
By further addition of N-GQD the interface is saturated and repulsive force between the particles increases, balancing the adsorption and repulsion forces and flattening the surface tension at the 0.01 to 0.1 wt% of N-GQD.
III)
Finally, the surface tension approaches the steady value due to the saturation of N-GQD at the adsorbed layer. Adding extra particles, decreases the surface tension until 1 wt% of N-GQD.

Figure S4 exhibits the STDE versus N-GQD concentrations that highlight the local maxima at 0.1 wt%, which is consistent with the plateau's ends that occurred at the 0.1 wt% of N-GQD. After this point, it could be inferred that the interface is fully saturated and the surface is restructured. The lower surface energy is caused by the electrostatic repulsion force of particles[36].. By adding more N-GQDs nanoparticle, the solubilization from the interface to bulk occurs while the interface's elasticity encounters a stable constant trend. As the concentration of N-GQDs meets 0.1 wt.% it turns out that specified N-GQDs molecular magnitude occupied the interface; it is worth mentioning that the equilibrium surface tension trend and plateau verify this behavior (Fig. 2).

Decreased surface tension and enhanced elasticity values of pure N-GQDs (Fig. 2) definitely guaranteed the N-GQDs existence at the interface and consequently illustrates that this nanoparticle is surface active. As the N-GQDs concentration is low, in consequence of diluted adsorption layer, throughout the drop oscillation period, the surface tension changes are limited. The flattened elasticity plateau of N-GQDs solutions exhibits that there might be a critical N-GQDs molecule number needed to be adsorbed and saturate the air/water interface. Immediately upon the saturation happens, the plateau of elasticity is appeared due to the maximal N-GQDs presence at the interface.

The most concluding results of this section declare that: it is obvious that N-GQDs adsorption has an optimum weight percent (0.1 wt% of N-GQD) for the effective interface modification, which can be utilized for surface properties tuning [8,9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic interfacial tension of CTAB and N­GQDs mixture
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned earlier, CTAB/ N­GQDs mixture interfacial behavior evaluated using the tensiometry device of pendant drop in both equilibrium surface pressure measurement and dynamic surface tension experiments. All the stable sonicated samples were examined for their surface properties measurements. Fig. 3 depicts the dynamic surface tension containing 0.01 to 0.3 wt% of N­GQD mixed with the CTAB concentrations of 0.1, 0.25 and 1 CMC (0.092, 0.23 and 0.92 mM), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 (A, B & C), when the solution system was free of nanoparticles (pure surfactant), the surface tension is quickly decreased, which could be inferred that the monomers of CTAB get themselves rapidly on to the drop surface. It is worth mentioning that CTAB monomer diffusion starts when the droplet is created in the cuvette before the measurements starts. Anyhow, surface tension arrives at the equilibrium state in about 50 s. The interfacial tension reduction for each mixture from its primary value can be assigned to the gradual CTAB & CTAB/N­GQDs mixtures adsorption to the interface. It should be noted that in some samples containing N­GQDs, the initial surface tension starts slightly higher than 72 mN/m (i.e. pure water surface tension). This trend expresses the notion that the share of free surfactant monomers is inferior in these mixtures. Consequently, they could not contribute to initial surface tension reduction that it would have been anticipated, like pure CTAB samples. From then on, the surface tension decreases, invariably approaching its equilibrium state in a much longer time (>1000s, regarding CTAB/N­GQDs ratio). As it is clear in Fig. 3, the mixtures containing N­GQDs/CTAB demonstrate reduced equilibrium interfacial tension, implying that N­GQDs/CTAB mixtures cause extreme adsorption in the air/water interface.

In Fig. 3A, B and C, the pure surfactant surface tension curve is shown at concentrations lower than 1 CMC. In each graph lower CMC values are shown as yellow and high CMC values are shown as red line to highlight the upper and lower limit of diagrams. This makes it easier to track the trends and compare adsorption dynamic properties such as adsorption rate and consequently the surface tension reduction rate and elasticity of particle / surfactant systems. Moreover, the changes in surface tension of a mixed surfactant/ particle dispersions are not necessarily monotonic, and depends on the molecular ratio of particle to surfactant (Table 2 ). As shown in Fig. 3A at 0.1 CMC of surfactant the lowest surface tension at 0.1 wt% of N-GQD occurred with the slowest dynamic of adsorption and the highest response to the sinusoidal perturbation, implying the more surface-active mixture adsorption at the interface. However, by addition of more particles at the specified (0.1 CMC) surfactant concentration to 0.3 wt% of N-GQDs, the share of surfactant to each nanoparticle sheet decreases which result in lower hydrophobicity and surface activity of the mixture. Therefore, the surface tension reduction for 0.3 wt% sample is not as obvious as 0.1 wt% sample (Fig. 3A). These findings are also verified by Zeta potential measurements in Section 3.2.

Fig. 3 proves that the mixed nanoparticle/ surfactant surface tension may decrease to significantly lower values than the pure surfactant solution. This unique trend can be associated with the N­GQDs higher concentration of the systems, which leads to the higher number of mixtures residing at the air/water interface. Additionally, CTAB addition to the N­GQDs solution will decline the charge repulsion of quantum dots nanoparticle at the air/water interface and simultaneously increase the hydrophobicity of the particles, which causes more favorable adsorption of CTAB/N­GQDs mixtures to the interface (Fig. 3B, C). According to Fig. 3B at the 0.25 CMC of CTAB the surfactant monomer is high enough, and formed mixtures are surface-active with sufficient hydrophobicity. So, as the concentration of N-GQD increase to 0.3 wt% the surface tension decreases monotonically.

Moreover, by adding N-GQD to 1 wt%, the concentration of particle compensates for the lack of enough surfactant concentration which is needed to reach saturation, thus the decreasing trend of surface tension continues. To address the mixed systems surface behavior and its effect on the CMC concentration the CMC concentration line is included in all subgraphs (please see Fig. 3B-1% N-GQD+0.25 CMC CTAB which its dynamic surface tension lies below pure CTAB line). At 1 CMC of CTAB as shown in Fig. 3C, the excess amount of CTAB molecules is present, and the adsorption on the N-GQD reduces surface tension relative to the pure surfactant. But, by the addition of N-GQD to 0.3 wt%, the adsorption dynamic is slower, and the elasticity response to the perturbation is heightened, which approves the surface-active mixture creation and adsorption.

Fig. 2 depicts that the surface tension of N-GQDs is set at about 56 mN/m for concentrations above 0.01 weight percent, signifying the cooperation at the air/water interface. Nevertheless, the excessive hydrophilicity and surface charge imply that the N-GQD particles do not accumulate substantially at the interface and therefore do not kinetically stabilize emulsions. The N-GQDs hydrophilicity disclose their weak surface activity on the surface, and as a result, they do not give the interface adequate stability to emulsify N-GQDs effectively [14]. Adsorption of N-GQD to the interface is therefore unfavorable. In the case of CTAB, the N−GQDs appear to have spontaneous adsorption at the interface by adding surface-active materials (which, in our case CTAB), developing mixtures with unusual surface activity.

The CTAB / N-GQD equilibrium surface tension is measured versus concentrations of CTAB at five distinct N-GQD concentrations (Figure S5). All the mixtures of CTAB / N­GQDs reflect an identical form of surface tension plots. It appears that N­GQDs nanoparticles with a medium tendency to adsorb at the interface are more hydrophobic due to the CTAB adsorption. This behavior is attributed to the CTAB hydrophobic tail exposure to the bulk of liquid, which is demonstrated in Fig. 4 . Accordingly, N­GQDs nanoparticles, which possess higher hydrophobic properties, appear to be adsorbed at the interface. Since each N­GQDs individual particle transports several CTAB molecules, it serves as a surface-active component. Thus, surface tension is reduced due to the interchange between nanoparticle surface and the air/ water interface.

At higher NGQDs concentration, higher amount of nanoparticle/surfactant mixtures stay in the bulk of solution which readily migrates to the surface, and therefore reduce the surface tension. The larger CTAB/N­GQDs mixture size compared to the CTAB surfactant molecule size result in a lower N­GQDs diffusion coefficient which leads to the time delay to reach on the interface and decreases the surface tension in comparison with the pure surfactant molecules.

Fig. 4 shows the particle and surfactant adsorption mechanisms in pure surfactant and pure nanoparticle (No. 1 and 2 of Fig. 4) and mixed CTAB/ N-GQD (No. 3 to 6 of Fig. 4), respectively. The surface tension results of pure particles showed that they are surface-active and adsorbed on the air/water interface like surfactants. The elasticity and STDE data confirmed this behavior. The adsorption mechanism of the particle/surfactant mixed dispersion can be investigated in three different cases.

First, the concentration of surfactant relative to the particle is low. In this case, all the surfactant monomers are adsorbed on the surface of particles. As a result of this adsorption, the molecular ratio of surfactant/particle would be low, so the free surfactant would not remain in bulk. Thus, hydrophobicity and surface activity of the CTAB/NGQD mixture would not be high enough for stable adsorption onto the air/liquid interface (Fig. 4 No.3). Second, when the concentration of surfactant relative to the particle is sufficient, and the surface of the particles is saturated with a surfactant, and they become surface active enough to readily adsorbed on the interface and cause surface tension reduction and elasticity improvement (No. 4 and 5 of Fig. 4). Third, in this case, although there is excess surfactant leading to the high surface-active mixture creation which is easily adsorbed on the interface. Although, the surface tension reduction is not as much as the pure surfactant, but the elasticity can exhibit higher values. If the CTAB adsorbed in the double layer, the hydrophilic and bulky complex would have formed and may travel back into the bulk of the liquid (No. 6 of Fig. 4). It is worth noting that the adsorption of CTAB renders the air water interface artificially positively charged, which could also drive the adsorption of the Q-dots, besides making the particle more hydrophobic.

For a better understanding of the phenomenon and making a more accurate judgment on the N­GQDs adsorption behavior in a mixed surfactant/ nanoparticle system, a unique experimental protocol was organized. As the first step, dynamic pendant drop tests were carried out for about 2000 s, and during this interval, the hybrid system was given enough time to reach its equilibrium surface tension. Afterward, the volume of the droplet was gone through high linear amplitude, approximately 80 percent of the primary drop surface area, undertaking the oscillations of compression/expansion at a 1.5 × 10−6 ml s − 1 rate, and then the surface tension was recorded [12].

Fig. 5a illustrates the variations of droplet volume during the programmed oscillation protocol accompany the surface tension feedbacks measurement for the hybrid system of nanoparticles and surfactant. An isotherm of surface pressure (Fig. 5b) for the CTAB/N­GQDs mixture was also obtained using this dynamic tensiometry. As depicted in this figure, the surface pressure increasing occurred as the droplet volume is decreased. Therefore, CTAB/N­GQDs mixtures concentration has been increased at the air/water interface, and it exhibits irreversible adsorption, as expected for small sheet nanoparticles.

The described approach includes contracting the droplet area of the CTAB/N­GQDs dispersion, and hence this lowering of the drop volume provokes the elevation of CTAB/N­GQDs concentration at the interface. The findings confirm that the device does not keep up a persistent surface tension during the contraction, specifying the irreversible adsorption of CTAB/N­GQDs mixture at the interface. Thus these mixtures fail to re-distributing into the drop bulk [37].

In Fig. 5b, surface pressure values are outlined in the configuration of a hysteresis graph, which additionally justifies the assumption of mixture adsorption to the interface. At the same time, alteration in the sample drop area has been taken place. This graph's x-axis depicts the ratio of the actual surface area of a droplet to the initial value of this parameter. Materials showing elastic behavior at the interface commonly exhibit a forward in time the contraction line, as γ0 surface tension is recovered. Fully inelastic surfaces indicate a rapid γ0 recovery since the surface is not covered with mixtures [38,39].

The CTAB/N­GQD complexes present in-between characteristics including plastic/elastic behavior, which is system-specific, implying interface coverage with CTAB/N­GQD mixtures. This phenomenon mechanically resists the surface area downturn, and in consequence, surface pressure increased. Furthermore, there is a surface pressure rise when the droplet size reduces. Nevertheless, when the droplet expanded, the γ0 recovery is detected. This behavior may be attributed to mixtures' resilience at the interface and the CTAB/N­GQD mixtures' suitability to sustain straining at the interface. It is worth mentioning that the exerted deformation loads are convertible.

As mentioned in the literature [11,12,16], the surface tension changes during oscillation of drop volume which gives the surface elasticity modulus [40] (E= (dγ/dlnA)). The measured elasticity which is investigated in this research are expressed in Fig. 6 versus the number of CTAB molecules (NCTAB) to N-GQDs nanoparticles number (NN-GQDs)

The N-GQDs number is estimated by assuming the N-GQDs size of 5 nm from HR-TEM test in Figure S1c. The thickness of N-GQDs nanosheet is considered to be 0.5 nm estimated by the SPM elevation profile of Figure S1d (N-GQDs density = 1.8 g/cm3) [24]. The elasticity results confirm that at the various concentrations of N-GQDs, at the NCTAB/ NN-GQDs ratio < 20, elasticity values touch their highest value. In this case the free surfactant number reaches to almost zero. These findings portray exceptional elasticity data for CTAB/N-GQDs solutions compared to pure CTAB, 57 mN/m for 0.3 wt.% N-GQDs in the presence of 0.1 CMC CTAB.

Fig. 2 shows the significant impact of pure N-GQDs nanoparticles on the surface properties enhancement. For pure CTAB samples, the elasticity is about 3 to 14 mN/m (pink graph), depending on the CTAB weight percent. The elasticity is much higher for the CTAB/N-GQDs mixtures of 10 to 60 mN/m depending on the molecular ratio of CTAB/N-GQDs.

At the minimum concentration of CTAB (0.1 CMC), augmentation of the surface elasticity is significant, while adding extra surfactant bring about a slow elasticity addition. Moreover, these findings also signify the attendance of bulky CTAB/N-GQDs mixtures at the interface that can cause strong elasticity values.

As declared earlier, nanoparticles/ surfactant mixtures with extreme hydrophobic nature are adsorbed at the interface abruptly. Nevertheless, at the extra N-GQDs weight percent (0.3 wt.%), other mixtures are generated in bulk. Still, their surface activity is declined, which is attributed to the lower surfactant to nanoparticle ratio.

The CTAB/N-GQDs mixtures are bulkier than the surfactant molecule size; therefore, the nanoparticle mixture diffusion coefficient is reduced. The low diffusion constant is principal proof of increased elasticity values in the mixed systems.

Compared with our previous work [1] on the GO/SDS system, pure N-GQDs and N-GQDs/CTAB have higher air/water surface activity and surface elasticity, probably due to the smaller size of N-GQDs (∼1/80 of GO size). Lower size of particles facilitates nanoparticle and mixtures migration to the interface. The N-group on the surface of N-GQDs and particle-surfactant opposite-charge system balances the particle surface charge and hydrophobicity. As shown in Table 1, the adsorption of CTAB on the surface of the N-GQD alters the hydrophilic nature of N-GQD into a partially hydrophobic. The viscoelasticity measurements confirm the creation of these hydrophobic mixtures of N-GQDs/CTAB and its adsorption on the interface.

To sum up, pure N-dopped graphene quantum dot's and oppositely charged N-GQDs/CTAB surface behavior at air/water interface is quantified versus N-GQDs and CTAB concentration using dynamic surface tension and elasticity measurements. N-GQD exhibited surface-active behavior and had a meaningful impact on the values of the air/water surface tension and viscoelasticity. The N-GQDs/CTAB ratios determine the driving force of the N-GQDs/CTAB mixtures' adsorption onto the interface and travelling back into the bulk. The results validated that as far as the amphiphilic surface-active N-GQDs are partially hydrophobic, they expose an immense tendency to be adsorbed at the air/water interface. Too many CTAB monomers help out the N-GQDs over-coating and stimulate them to travel back into the solution bulk. This is anticipated due to the nanoparticle-surfactant heavy complex size, which is equivalent to a much smaller diffusion coefficient. The elasticity of CTAB/N-GQDs system is hugely enhanced, these findings were likely due to the impact of N-GQDs on the interface behavior which was shown to be highly viscoelastic. This research highlight that selecting the right ratio of CTAB/N-GQDs is an appealing method of adequately tuning their intrinsic interfacial behavior and exploiting new phenomena for advanced chemical, oil and gas applications of sheet like nanoparticles. Besides, the N-GQDs and its complex intrinsic self-assembly nature makes these materials a potential additive for aqueous foam stabilization, so in cosmetic, food and oil recovery and etc. applications

Hadis Koolivand: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Saeedeh Mazinani: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Farhad Sharif: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2023.102714.
